# The Quiet Hour

The data center was always coldest just before dawn. The HVAC system
kept the air at sixty-six degrees year-round, but at four in the
morning, with the racks idling between batch jobs, it felt closer to
sixty. Mara pulled her hoodie tighter and walked the long aisle
between rows seven and eight, her badge clattering against her chest
with each step.

She liked the quiet hour. The rest of the night-shift crew had
filtered into the break room for coffee an hour ago, and Mara had
stayed behind, finishing her tour. The fans were a wash of pink
noise; the LEDs along the rack tops blinked their reassuring green.
Once, in row twelve, she had heard a fan blade catch a foreign
object and shriek for almost a full minute before tripping the
thermal cutout. She had filed the incident report, called the
vendor, and replaced the fan herself before the morning standup. The
report was three sentences long. The vendor's apology, delivered by
email at noon, was four pages.

Tonight, the only anomaly was a single drive in rack 7B blinking
amber instead of green. Mara crouched in front of it, ran the bay
diagnostic from her tablet, and waited. The diagnostic said what she
had already suspected: predicted failure within ninety days. She
tagged the bay for swap-out, wrote a one-line note in the runbook
ticket, and stood up.

At the end of the row, she paused and looked back. Two hundred and
forty drives, in eighty bays, in twelve racks. Every one of them
serving someone, somewhere, who would never know her name. She liked
that part too.

The coffee in the break room was bad and would be cold by now. She
turned toward it anyway, the badge clattering, the fans still humming
in their pink-noise wash, and let the quiet hour close behind her.
